summary:
The adolescent presents with delayed skeletal maturation evidenced by a bone age of 11 versus chronic age of 14, along with absence of axillary/pubic hair despite early breast development—a hallmark feature of constitutional growth delay (CGD). This condition typically results in catch-up growth over time and leads to an ultimately normal adult height, making reassurance and periodic observation the most suitable management approach.

The patient’s findings—including delayed bone age, lack of secondary sex characteristics despite stage 2 breast development—are consistent with constitutional growth delay rather than familial short stature. Constitutional growth delay usually resolves spontaneously with eventual catch-up growth leading to typical adult heights, so ongoing surveillance instead of intervention is recommended. 

Only include information directly related to diagnosis and management based on clinical presentation provided above.

We are asked only to summarize the agent's reasoning into exactly TWO SENTENCES focusing solely on KEY CLINICAL POINTS RELEVANT TO THE QUESTION.

Final summary:

The patient exhibits delayed bone age (11 vs. 14 years) and absent pubertal features (no axillary/pubic hair) despite breast development—atypical for familial short stature—and these patterns align with constitutional growth delay. Management should involve reassurance and routine follow-up as CGD generally allows for spontaneous catch-up growth resulting in normal ultimate height.
